critique.summary: The article claims that the Cyber.mil website is serving file downloads using a TLS certificate that expired three days ago. However, the content provided shows a listing of STIG documents with future upload dates, suggesting the site is operational and updated.
mainPoints: Cyber.mil allegedly uses an expired TLS certificate for file downloads.
The article provides a list of STIG documents with upload dates in 2026.
The site appears to be actively maintained with recent document uploads.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that Cyber.mil is using an expired TLS certificate cannot be verified from the provided content, which only shows a document listing with future dates. External verification via current web security tools or certificate checkers is necessary to confirm the certificate status. The presence of future-dated documents suggests the site is active and maintained, which contradicts the claim of an expired certificate causing issues.
considerations: TLS certificate status should be verified using real-time SSL/TLS inspection tools or browsers.
The article does not provide evidence such as screenshots or certificate details to support the claim.
Future upload dates in the document list may be placeholders or errors and should be cross-checked.
The impact of an expired certificate on user access and security is not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the expired TLS certificate on cyber.mil, highlighting operational failures, security risks, and the implications of instructing users to bypass certificate warnings. Several commenters analyze the technical and organizational shortcomings behind the issue.

comments.author: amluto
comments.text:
This is kind of amazing. I'm suspicious that the site operator has absolutely no idea what they're doing.

> DoD Cyber Exchange site is undergoing a TSSL Certification renewal

I'm imagining someone searching around for a consulting or testing company that will help them get a personal TSSL Certification, whatever that is (a quick search suggests that it does not exist, as one would expect). And perhaps they have no idea what TLS is or how any modern WebPKI works, which is extra amazing, since cyber.mil is apparently a government PKI provider (see the top bar).

Of course, the DoD realized that their whole web certificate system was incompatible with ordinary browsers and they wrote a memo (which you have to click past the certificate error to read):

https://dl.dod.cyber.mil/wp-content/uploads/pki-pke/pdf/uncl...

saying that, through February 2024, unclassified DoD sites are permitted to use ordinary commercial CAs.

If the DoD were remotely competent at this sort of thing, they would (a) have CAA records (because their written policy does nothing whatsoever to tell the CA/B-compliant CAs of the world not to issue .mil certificates, (b) run their own intermediate CA that had a signature from a root CA (or was even a root CA itself), and (c) use automatically-renewed short-lived certificates for the actual websites.

cyber.mil currently uses IdenTrust, which claims to be DoD approved. They also, ahem, claim to support ACME:

> In support of the broader CA community, IdenTrust—through HID and the acquisition of ZeroSSL—actively contributes to the development and maintenance of major open-source ACME clients, including Caddy Server and ACME.sh. These efforts help promote accessibility, interoperability, and automation in certificate management.

Err... does that mean that they actually support ACME on their DoD-approved certificates or does that mean that they bought some companies that participate in the ACME ecosystem? (ACME is not amazing except in contrast to what came before and as an exercise in getting something reasonable deployed in a very stodgy ecosystem, but ACME plus a well-designed DNS-01 implementation plus CAA can be very secure.)

The offending certificate is:

    Certificate:
        Data:
            Version: 3 (0x2)
            Serial Number:
                40:01:95:b4:87:b3:a3:a9:12:e0:d7:21:f8:b3:91:61
            Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
            Issuer: C=US, O=IdenTrust, OU=TrustID Server, CN=TrustID Server CA O1
            Validity
                Not Before: Mar 20 17:09:07 2025 GMT
                Not After : Mar 20 17:08:07 2026 GMT
            Subject: C=US, ST=Maryland, L=Fort Meade, O=DEFENSE INFORMATION SYSTEMS AGENCY, CN=public.cyber.mil
At least the site uses TLS 1.3.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, knowledgeable critique of the DoD's certificate management practices, referencing official memos and technical details, making it highly accurate and insightful.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
> Users on civilian network can continue downloads through the Advance tab in the error message.

They are literally telling users to click through the browser errors about the bad cert. They don't mention that there is a very specific error they should be looking for (expired cert). This gives any MITMer the opportunity right now to replace downloaded executables with malware-laden ones using nothing more than a self-signed cert and a proxy. You can bet your boots China, NK, Iran, Russia are all having a good laugh. Biggest military in the world and they can't get a web server working.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly highlights the significant security risk of instructing users to bypass certificate warnings, emphasizing the potential for man-in-the-middle attacks.

comments.author: yesod
comments.text: So it looks like a new cert was issued back in February, but they've not deployed it yet (https://bgp.he.net/certs#_SearchTab?q=www.public.cyber.mil)
comments.analysis: This comment provides factual information about the certificate issuance timeline, supporting the claim that the new certificate exists but is not yet deployed.

comments.author: kevincloudsec
comments.text: telling users on a cybersecurity website to click past certificate warnings is training them to do the exact thing every security awareness program says never to do. DISA runs the security standards that every defense contractor has to comply with...
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out the contradiction and risk in encouraging users to ignore security warnings, especially on a security-focused site.

comments.author: driftnet
comments.text: Inexcusable but should clarify that cyber.mil and public.cyber.mil are actually different things. Most people downloading from the site are not using public.cyber.mil, so maybe they care less? This is still one of those highly-visible things that is going to bring down the heat quickly, so it's just dumb to let it happen.
comments.analysis: This comment adds useful context about domain differences, which may affect the scope of the problem, though it does not excuse the issue.

comments.author: petcat
comments.text: Is there anything inherently insecure about an expired cert other than your browser just complaining about it?
comments.analysis: This question shows curiosity and partially misunderstands the risks; expired certificates undermine trust and can expose users to attacks.

comments.author: nik282000
comments.text:
TD bank, in Canada, has had their cert expire several times in the past 10 years.

It blows me away that a bank can't afford to do for themselves what Certbot and Lets Encrypt does for me, for free.

Like, pay a guy a whole week to automate this and it will save you the 12hrs losses every time your cert expires.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a relatable example of certificate expiry issues and advocates for automation, which is a practical and accurate suggestion.

comments.author: dmitrygr
comments.text: So what? They keep shortening the validity length of these certificates, making them more and more of a pain to deal with.
comments.analysis: While valid about shorter certificate lifetimes increasing management overhead, this comment lacks depth and ignores the security benefits of shorter validity.

comments.author: bilekas
comments.text:
> DoD Cyber Exchange site is undergoing a TSSL Certification renewal

TSSL renewal does not cause downtime.. If it's actually done of course.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly notes that certificate renewal should not cause downtime, implying operational failure if downtime occurs.

comments.author: supermatt
comments.text: Clearly this is some advanced cyber-warfare technique intended to cause adversaries tools to fail with an "expired certificate" error...
comments.analysis: This sarcastic comment is likely trolling and does not contribute factual or constructive insight.
